revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Amenohiboko]][[fa:آمه‌نوهیبوکو]][[fr:Amenohiboko]][[id:Amenohiboko]][[ja:アメノヒボコ]][[ko:아메노히보코]][[zh:天日槍]] {{Infobox deity|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|consort={{ill|Akaruhime|qid=Q555212|1=Akaruhime-no-Kami}}|relatives={{ill|Hyeokgeose of Silla|qid=Q484898|1=Hyeokgeose of Silla}}; {{ill|Ii Tomoyasu|qid=Q11161082|1=Ii Tomoyasu}}; {{ill|Tajimamori|qid=Q11316133|1=Tajimamori}}}}
[[File:Statue of Amenohiboko.png|thumb|Statue of Amenohiboko in {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|qid=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture|1=Fukui Prefecture}}, [[:en:Tsuruga City|Tsuruga City]]]] {{nihongo|'''Amenohiboko'''|天日槍|qq=}} was a legendary prince of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|qid=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}} who settled in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} during the era of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|qid=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}}, around the 3rd or 4th century<ref>[[Nihon Shoki]], Vol.6 "天日槍對曰 僕新羅國主之子也 然聞日本國有聖皇 則以己國授弟知古而化歸之"</ref> and was said to have lived in {{ill|Tajima Province|en|Tajima Province|qid=Q1053727|lt=Tajima Province|1=Tajima Province}}. His descendants are the [[Tajima clan]].<ref>[[Nihon Shoki]], Vol.6 "故天日槍娶但馬出嶋人 太耳女麻多烏 生但馬諸助也 諸助生但馬日楢杵 日楢杵生清彦清彦生田道間守也"</ref> Amenohiboko is the ancestral god of {{ill|Tajima Province|en|Tajima Province|qid=Q1053727|lt=Tajima Province|1=Tajima Province}} and is supposedly enshrined in the {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} (''[[:en:Izushi jinja|Izushi jinja]]'') at {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|en|Toyooka, Hyōgo|qid=Q696388|lt=Toyooka|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}.<ref>TOYOOKA City Hyōgo Prefecture [Travel Guide] [http://www.city.toyooka.hyogo.jp/english/travel/each/sight/02cultural.html] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110612021414/http://www.city.toyooka.hyogo.jp/english/travel/each/sight/02cultural.html|date=2011-06-12|qq=}} "“Amenohiboko”, the ancestral god of the Tajima area is enshrined and eight kinds of treasure are present as Tajima's best shrine. Its name appears listed in the oldest literature of Japan, the Kojiki- and Nihon-shoki ("The Description of Folk History" and "The Chronicle of Japan," written in the Nara era), and is said to have been an important shrine of the San-in area since ancient days."</ref> Seven or eight treasures brought by Amenohiboko are thought to be housed in [[Izushi Shrine]] in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', "In Kagami Village, [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]], there was a craftsman of Suebe who served the prince of Silla, Amenohiboko, who came to Japan."<ref>{{cite web|last1=Kanzaki|first1=Shiho|title=The history of Shigaraki pottery|url=http://www.the-anagama.com/En/articles/pot_history.html|publisher=www.the-anagama.com|accessdate=26 August 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303193211/http://www.the-anagama.com/En/articles/pot_history.html|archive-date=3 March 2016|url-status=dead|qq=}}</ref><ref>The Suinin Chronicle of [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]] "是以近江国鏡谷陶人。則天日槍之従人也"</ref> However, at present, early Sueki was not found at Kagamiyama ruins of old kilns in Ryuocho, Shiga Prefecture, which is closely linked to this description, or either in Tajima region where Amenohiboko is said to have lived. Amenohiboko is attributed, in legend, some parts of {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|en|Toyooka, Hyōgo|qid=Q696388|lt=Toyooka City|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}.<ref>{{cite web|title=Introduction|url=http://www.city.toyooka.hyogo.jp/english/introduction/history/index.html|publisher=www.city.toyooka.hyogo.jp|accessdate=26 August 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110612021356/http://www.city.toyooka.hyogo.jp/english/introduction/history/index.html|archive-date=2011-06-12|url-status=dead|qq=}}</ref> His descendant became the mother to {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}. == History == [[Image:Izushi Jinja 03.JPG|thumb|225px|Izushi Shrine|left]] According to the ''[[Kojiki]]'', a woman was laying down near a {{ill|swamp|en|swamp|qid=Q166735|lt=swamp|1=Swamp}} called "Anuguma (阿具奴摩/阿具沼)" in Silla, where the {{ill|Sunlight|en|Sunlight|qid=Q193788|lt=Sunlight|1=Sunlight}} touched her {{ill|vagina|en|vagina|qid=Q5880|lt=vagina|1=Vagina}} and she gave birth to a red ball on the spot. A passerby witnessed the event and pleaded her to give the ball to him where he finally took possession of it. One day, the man was walking with his {{ill|Cow|en|Cow|qid=Q11748378|lt=Cow|1=Cow}} to deliver some provisions when Amenohiboko (then a Silla prince) saw him and mistook him for trying to eat the cow. After being imprisoned, the man begged to be set free, offering the red ball as compensation. After accepting the offer, Amenohiboko brought the red ball home where it turned into a fully grown woman of great beauty. The prince decided to marry the woman and the two lived relatively happily until Amenohiboko lashed out at his newly wedded wife for a trivial reason. The woman became upset and stated that she was going to return to her "homeland", then got on a small boat and sailed to {{ill|Namba|en|Namba|qid=Q1075406|lt=Namba|1=Namba}}. Realizing his mistake and seeking for forgiveness, Amenohiboko set sail to Namba himself, but the guardian [[Kami]] prevented him from entering the land and he had to stop in {{ill|Tajima Province|en|Tajima Province|qid=Q1053727|lt=Tajima|1=Tajima Province}} where he stayed. He then found another wife named "Maemitsu (前津見)".<ref name="古事記p.275-278">『新編日本古典文学全集 1 古事記』小学館、2004th year （[[ジャパンナレッジ]]版）、pp. 275-278。</ref> The original wife who had arrived in Namba became a deity to its people and was called "Akaruhime (阿加流比売)".<ref name="古事記p.275-278" /> === Interpretation === Scholars have compared the story of Akaruhime's birth with the red ball to other similar legends that were told in the region, the most similar stories being [[:en:Chumo of Goguryeo|Chumo of Goguryeo]], {{ill|Suro of Geumgwan Gaya|en|Suro of Geumgwan Gaya|qid=Q496836|lt=Suro of Geumgwan Gaya|1=Suro of Geumgwan Gaya}} and {{ill|Hyeokgeose of Silla|en|Hyeokgeose of Silla|qid=Q484898|lt=Hyeokgeose of Silla|1=Hyeokgeose of Silla}} all being born from an egg.<ref>The story mentions "玉" which could be interpreted as both "ball" and "egg".</ref> They point to Chumo's story in particular where his mother, {{ill|Lady Yuhwa|en|Lady Yuhwa|qid=Q717467|lt=Lady Yuhwa|1=Lady Yuhwa}} (daughter of the river god {{ill|Habaek|en|Habaek|qid=Q55613267|lt=Habaek|1=Habaek}}) was touched by the sunlight on to her private parts where she became pregnant with an egg which later hatched into Chumo. Historians claim that such repertoire in Asian mythology is common between Siberian to northeastern civilizations and that the story of Amenohiboko and Akaruhime was heavily influenced by Korean legends of the same premise (both individuals originating from Silla). Another theory points to the similarities between Amenohiboko and the story of {{ill|Yeonorang and Seonyeo|en|Yeonorang and Seonyeo|qid=Q12607139|lt=Yeonorang and Seonyeo|1=Yeonorang and Seonyeo}}. This theory revolves around the involvement of individuals of Silla unintentionally immigrating to Japan with heavy elements alluding to the importance of the {{ill|Sun|en|Sun|qid=Q525|lt=Sun|1=Sun}}.<ref name="ekfc">{{cite encyclopedia |year= |title=연오랑세오녀(延乌郎细乌女) |encyclopedia=[[Encyclopedia of Korean Folk Culture]] |publisher=[[National Folk Museum of Korea]] |location=서울 |id= |url=https://folkency.nfm.go.kr/kr/topic/detail/5585 |access-date=2022-11-30 |last=송 |first=효섭 |date=|qq=}}</ref> Though the possibilities of Yeono and Seo becoming the king and queen of Japan are highly unlikely, the chances of immigrants with great importance becoming royalty were not uncommon, and like the story of Yeonorang and Seonyeo, the story of Amenohiboko was also deemed similar. == Legacy == His descendant became the mother to {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}, a controversial queen who allegedly invaded and conquered the self-declared "promised land", often interpreted as the [[:en:Korean peninsula|Korean peninsula]]. World-renowned linguist and Japanese language expert {{ill|Alexander Vovin|en|Alexander Vovin|qid=Q848595|lt=Alexander Vovin|1=Alexander Vovin}} state that due to Amenohiboko being of Korean origin, Jingū and her son and successor, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} might have been native speakers of the {{ill|Korean language|en|Korean language|qid=Q9176|lt=Korean language|1=Korean language}}.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Vovin |first=Alexander |title=Immigrants or Overlords? Korean Influences on Japan in the Archaic Period: a Linguistic Perspective |date=2012-06-14 |publisher=Institut für Kulturund Geitestesgeschichte Asiens |pages=29 |language=en|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Quote|text="According to the legend, future Emperor Ōjin was born in Kyūshū after Empress Jingū returned from her military expedition to Korea. It is highly doubtful that he was given a Korean name in order to honor the defeated enemies. This fact brings us to a reasonable suspicion that both Ōjin and Jingū were actually native speakers of Korean."|author=Alexander Vovin (2012)|title=Immigrants or Overlords? Korean Influences on Japan in the Archaic Period: a Linguistic Perspective|qq=}} </ref> == See also == * {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}} * {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} * {{ill|Yeonorang and Seonyeo|en|Yeonorang and Seonyeo|qid=Q12607139|lt=Yeonorang and Seonyeo|1=Yeonorang and Seonyeo}} ==Notes== {{Reflist|qq=}}
[[en:Amenohiboko]]
<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:آمه‌نوهیبوکو]] [[fr:Amenohiboko]] [[id:Amenohiboko]] [[ja:アメノヒボコ]] [[ko:아메노히보코]] [[zh:天日槍]] {{wikidata link|Q713100}} [[Category:People of Tajima Province]] [[Category:History of Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Pre-modern Koreans in Japan]] [[Category:Pre-modern Korean-Japanese people]] [[Category:古代日朝関係]] [[Category:Silla people]] [[Category:Deified Japanese people in the Kiki]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Toraijin]]
